Description: Each composable should have a single responsibility for better testability and reuse
Do: Extract complex composables into smaller focused functions
Don't: Create composables with hundreds of lines
Code Good: @Composable fun TraderCard(trader: Trader) { Card { Column { AvatarImage(trader.avatar); TraderStats(trader.stats) } } }
Code Bad: @Composable fun MainScreen() { /* 500 lines of UI code */ }

Description: Name composables descriptively using PascalCase to indicate their purpose
Do: Name composables after their function like TraderRankingList
Don't: Use generic names like MyComposable or Screen1
Code Good: @Composable fun TraderPerformanceChart(data: PerformanceData) { ... }
Code Bad: @Composable fun Chart1(d: Any) { ... }

Description: Stateless composables are easier to test preview and reuse
Do: Hoist state to callers and accept state and events as parameters
Don't: Mix state management and UI in every composable
Code Good: @Composable fun TraderRow(trader: Trader, onFavorite: () -> Unit) { Row { Text(trader.name); IconButton(onClick = onFavorite) { Icon(Icons.Default.Star) } } }
Code Bad: @Composable fun TraderRow(traderId: String) { var trader by remember { mutableStateOf<Trader?>(null) } LaunchedEffect(traderId) { trader = api.getTrader(traderId) } }

Guideline: Accept Modifier as first optional parameter
Description: Consistently accepting Modifier allows callers to customize layout behavior
Do: Add modifier: Modifier = Modifier as first optional parameter
Don't: Hardcode layout properties that callers might need to override
Code Good: @Composable fun TraderCard(modifier: Modifier = Modifier, trader: Trader) { Card(modifier = modifier) { ... } }
Code Bad: @Composable fun TraderCard(trader: Trader) { Card(modifier = Modifier.fillMaxWidth()) { ... } }

Guideline: Use slot-based API for flexible composables
Description: Slot APIs allow callers to provide custom content increasing reusability
Do: Use composable lambdas for customizable content areas
Don't: Hardcode all content in reusable composables
Code Good: @Composable fun CardWithHeader(header: @Composable () -> Unit, content: @Composable () -> Unit) { Column { header(); content() } }
Code Bad: @Composable fun CardWithHeader(title: String, subtitle: String) { Column { Text(title); Text(subtitle) } }

Description: remember caches values across recompositions preventing recalculation
Do: Use remember for values that are expensive to compute
Don't: Recalculate expensive values on every recomposition
Code Good: val sortedTraders = remember(traders) { traders.sortedByDescending { it.roi } }
Code Bad: val sortedTraders = traders.sortedByDescending { it.roi } // Recalculated every recomposition

Description: rememberSaveable preserves state across configuration changes and process death
Do: Use rememberSaveable for user input that should survive rotation
Don't: Use remember for input state that gets lost on rotation
Code Good: var searchQuery by rememberSaveable { mutableStateOf('') }
Code Bad: var searchQuery by remember { mutableStateOf('') } // Lost on rotation

Description: Place state at the lowest common ancestor that needs it
Do: Hoist state to the level where it needs to be shared
Don't: Keep all state in the root composable
Code Good: @Composable fun ParentScreen() { var query by remember { mutableStateOf('') }; SearchBar(query = query, onQueryChange = { query = it }) }
Code Bad: @Composable fun SearchBar() { var query by remember { mutableStateOf('') } /* All state internal */ }

Description: derivedStateOf prevents unnecessary recompositions when source state changes frequently
Do: Use derivedStateOf when computing values from other state that changes often
Don't: Compute derived values directly causing unnecessary recompositions
Code Good: val highPerformers by remember { derivedStateOf { traders.filter { it.roi > 100 } } }
Code Bad: val highPerformers = traders.filter { it.roi > 100 } // Recomputes on any recomposition

Description: snapshotFlow converts Compose state to Flow for use with coroutines
Do: Use snapshotFlow when you need to react to state changes in coroutines
Don't: Create manual state observation patterns
Code Good: LaunchedEffect(Unit) { snapshotFlow { listState.firstVisibleItemIndex }.collect { index -> analytics.logScroll(index) } }
Code Bad: // Manual state checking in a loop

Description: LaunchedEffect runs suspend functions tied to composable lifecycle
Do: Use LaunchedEffect for one-time or key-based coroutine side effects
Don't: Launch coroutines without lifecycle management
Code Good: LaunchedEffect(traderId) { trader = repository.getTrader(traderId) }
Code Bad: // Launching coroutine without effect scope var trader by remember { mutableStateOf<Trader?>(null) } // Missing LaunchedEffect

Description: DisposableEffect handles setup and cleanup for effects that need disposal
Do: Use DisposableEffect when effect needs cleanup on disposal or key change
Don't: Forget to clean up observers or listeners
Code Good: DisposableEffect(lifecycleOwner) { val observer = LifecycleEventObserver { _, event -> ... }; lifecycleOwner.lifecycle.addObserver(observer); onDispose { lifecycleOwner.lifecycle.removeObserver(observer) } }
Code Bad: // Adding observer without removal

Description: SideEffect runs after every successful recomposition for non-suspend side effects
Do: Use SideEffect for logging or analytics that should run on each recomposition
Don't: Put non-compose code directly in composable body without SideEffect
Code Good: SideEffect { analytics.logScreenView(screenName) }
Code Bad: @Composable fun Screen() { analytics.logScreenView(screenName) /* Not in SideEffect */ }

Description: produceState converts async data into Compose State cleanly
Do: Use produceState when loading data that becomes State
Don't: Manually manage loading state and data fetching
Code Good: val traderState by produceState<UiState>(Loading, traderId) { value = try { Success(repo.getTrader(traderId)) } catch (e: Exception) { Error(e) } }
Code Bad: var isLoading by remember { mutableStateOf(true) }; var data by remember { mutableStateOf<Data?>(null) }; LaunchedEffect(key) { isLoading = true; data = fetch(); isLoading = false }

Description: Effect keys control when effects restart ensure they capture all dependencies
Do: Include all values that should trigger effect restart in the key
Don't: Use Unit or missing keys when effect should restart on value changes
Code Good: LaunchedEffect(traderId, sortOrder) { traders = repository.getTraders(traderId, sortOrder) }
Code Bad: LaunchedEffect(Unit) { traders = repository.getTraders(traderId, sortOrder) } // Won't restart when params change

Description: State flows down from parent to child and events flow up
Do: Pass state down and events up through the composable hierarchy
Don't: Allow children to directly modify parent state
Code Good: @Composable fun TraderList(traders: List<Trader>, onTraderClick: (Trader) -> Unit) { LazyColumn { items(traders) { trader -> TraderRow(trader, onClick = { onTraderClick(trader) }) } } }
Code Bad: // Child composables directly modifying ViewModel state

Description: ViewModel survives configuration changes and separates business logic from UI
Do: Use ViewModel for screen-level state management
Don't: Put business logic directly in composables
Code Good: @HiltViewModel class TraderListViewModel @Inject constructor(private val repo: TraderRepository) : ViewModel() { val traders: StateFlow<List<Trader>> = repo.getTraders().stateIn(viewModelScope, SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), emptyList()) }
Code Bad: @Composable fun TraderList() { var traders by remember { mutableStateOf(emptyList<Trader>()) }; LaunchedEffect(Unit) { traders = api.getTraders() } }

Guideline: Use StateFlow or State for UI state
Description: Expose UI state from ViewModel using StateFlow for reactive updates
Do: Use StateFlow with collectAsStateWithLifecycle for lifecycle-aware collection
Don't: Use LiveData in Compose without proper lifecycle handling
Code Good: val traders by viewModel.traders.collectAsStateWithLifecycle()
Code Bad: val traders by viewModel.tradersLiveData.observeAsState() // Less lifecycle-aware

Description: Create sealed classes or data classes to represent all UI states
Do: Define explicit states for loading error success and empty
Don't: Use multiple boolean flags for UI state
Code Good: sealed interface TraderListUiState { data object Loading : TraderListUiState; data class Success(val traders: List<Trader>) : TraderListUiState; data class Error(val message: String) : TraderListUiState }
Code Bad: var isLoading by mutableStateOf(false); var error by mutableStateOf<String?>(null); var traders by mutableStateOf(emptyList<Trader>())

Description: collectAsStateWithLifecycle stops collection when lifecycle is below threshold
Do: Use collectAsStateWithLifecycle for lifecycle-aware Flow collection
Don't: Use collectAsState which keeps collecting when in background
Code Good: val traders by viewModel.traders.collectAsStateWithLifecycle()
Code Bad: val traders by viewModel.traders.collectAsState() // Collects in background

Description: Ensure state survives configuration changes like rotation
Do: Use ViewModel and rememberSaveable for state that should survive rotation
Don't: Store important state only in remember
Code Good: // ViewModel state survives config changes @HiltViewModel class MyViewModel : ViewModel() { ... }
Code Bad: // State in remember lost on rotation var formData by remember { mutableStateOf(FormData()) }

Description: Understand that composables can be recomposed at any time
Do: Design composables to handle recomposition gracefully
Don't: Rely on composable body running only once
Code Good: LaunchedEffect(key) { /* Runs on first composition and when key changes */ }
Code Bad: @Composable fun Screen() { loadData() /* Runs on every recomposition */ }

Description: Navigation Compose provides type-safe navigation with proper back stack handling
Do: Use NavController and NavHost for navigation between screens
Don't: Manually manage navigation state with remember
Code Good: NavHost(navController, startDestination = 'traders') { composable('traders') { TraderListScreen() }; composable('trader/{id}') { TraderDetailScreen(it.arguments?.getString('id')) } }
Code Bad: var currentScreen by remember { mutableStateOf('home') } when (currentScreen) { 'home' -> HomeScreen() }

Description: Define type-safe routes and arguments for navigation
Do: Use sealed classes or type-safe builders for routes
Don't: Pass stringly-typed routes throughout the app
Code Good: sealed class Screen(val route: String) { data object Traders : Screen('traders'); data class TraderDetail(val id: String) : Screen('trader/$id') }
Code Bad: navController.navigate('trader/123') // Stringly typed everywhere

Description: Configure deep links for external navigation into your app
Do: Define deep link patterns in your navigation graph
Don't: Ignore deep linking requirements
Code Good: composable('trader/{id}', deepLinks = listOf(navDeepLink { uriPattern = 'app://trader/{id}' })) { TraderDetailScreen() }
Code Bad: // No deep link configuration

Description: Providing keys helps Compose track items correctly during reordering
Do: Use key composable for items in reorderable or changing lists
Don't: Let Compose use positional identity for dynamic lists
Code Good: LazyColumn { items(traders, key = { it.id }) { trader -> TraderRow(trader) } }
Code Bad: LazyColumn { items(traders) { trader -> TraderRow(trader) } } // No keys for dynamic list

Description: Read state as late as possible to minimize recomposition scope
Do: Use lambda parameters to defer state reads to composition
Don't: LazyColumn { items(100) { index -> val color by remember { derivedStateOf { if (index == selectedIndex.value) Color.Blue else Color.Gray } }; Item(color = color) } }
Code Good: val selected = selectedIndex.value; LazyColumn { items(100) { index -> Item(color = if (index == selected) Color.Blue else Color.Gray) } }
Code Bad: warning

Description: Stable types allow Compose to skip recomposition when inputs unchanged
Do: Use stable or immutable types for composable parameters
Don't: Pass unstable types that cause unnecessary recompositions
Code Good: @Immutable data class Trader(val id: String, val name: String, val roi: Double)
Code Bad: data class Trader(val id: String, val name: String, var roi: Double) // Mutable property makes unstable

Guideline: Use LazyColumn and LazyRow for lists
Description: Lazy layouts only compose visible items providing better performance
Do: Use Lazy layouts for lists with many items
Don't: Use Column with forEach for long lists
Code Good: LazyColumn { items(traders) { trader -> TraderRow(trader) } }
Code Bad: Column { traders.forEach { trader -> TraderRow(trader) } }

Description: Use Layout Inspector to identify recomposition issues
Do: Profile your composables to find unnecessary recompositions
Don't: Guess at performance issues without measurement
Code Good: // Use Android Studio Layout Inspector with recomposition counts enabled
Code Bad: // Adding remember everywhere without profiling

Description: MaterialTheme provides consistent theming across your app
Do: Access colors typography and shapes through MaterialTheme
Don't: Hardcode colors and styles throughout composables
Code Good: Text(text, color = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.primary, style = MaterialTheme.typography.bodyLarge)
Code Bad: Text(text, color = Color(0xFF1976D2), fontSize = 16.sp)

Description: Implement proper dark theme support for better accessibility
Do: Define both light and dark color schemes
Don't: Ignore dark theme or hardcode light colors
Code Good: MaterialTheme(colorScheme = if (isSystemInDarkTheme()) darkColorScheme() else lightColorScheme()) { ... }
Code Bad: MaterialTheme(colorScheme = lightColorScheme()) // No dark theme support

Description: Define colors by purpose not appearance for maintainable theming
Do: Use semantic names like surfaceContainer or primaryContainer
Don't: Use literal color names like blue400 or darkGray
Code Good: val backgroundColor = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.surface
Code Bad: val backgroundColor = Color(0xFFE0E0E0) // Magic color

Description: Choose the right layout composable for your needs
Do: Use Column Row Box for different layout patterns
Don't: Nest layouts unnecessarily or use wrong layout type
Code Good: Row(horizontalArrangement = Arrangement.SpaceBetween) { Text('Left'); Text('Right') }
Code Bad: Column { Row { Spacer(Modifier.weight(1f)); Text('Right') } } // Overcomplicated

Description: Weight distributes available space proportionally among siblings
Do: Use weight modifier for flexible space distribution
Don't: Use fixed sizes when proportional sizing is needed
Code Good: Row { Text('Fixed'); Spacer(Modifier.weight(1f)); Button(onClick = {}) { Text('Action') } }
Code Bad: Row { Text('Fixed'); Spacer(Modifier.width(200.dp)); Button(onClick = {}) { Text('Action') } }

Description: Account for system UI like status bar and navigation bar
Do: Use WindowInsets modifiers for proper inset handling
Don't: Ignore insets causing content to be hidden
Code Good: Scaffold(modifier = Modifier.windowInsetsPadding(WindowInsets.systemBars)) { ... }
Code Bad: Scaffold { ... } // Content may be behind system bars

Description: ConstraintLayout handles complex positioning without deep nesting
Do: Use ConstraintLayout for layouts with many interdependent positions
Don't: Create deeply nested Column and Row for complex layouts
Code Good: ConstraintLayout { val (title, subtitle, icon) = createRefs(); Text(Modifier.constrainAs(title) { top.linkTo(parent.top); start.linkTo(icon.end) }) }
Code Bad: Column { Row { Column { Row { /* Deep nesting */ } } } }

Description: Test composables to catch regressions and verify behavior
Do: Write UI tests for important composable functionality
Don't: Skip testing entirely
Code Good: @Test fun traderCard_showsTraderName() { composeTestRule.setContent { TraderCard(testTrader) }; composeTestRule.onNodeWithText('TestTrader').assertIsDisplayed() }
Code Bad: // No compose tests

Description: Find elements by semantic properties for resilient tests
Do: Use semantics-based matchers like onNodeWithText onNodeWithContentDescription
Don't: Find elements by implementation details
Code Good: composeTestRule.onNodeWithContentDescription('Favorite trader').performClick()
Code Bad: composeTestRule.onNode(hasTestTag('icon_button_3')).performClick() // Brittle

Description: Verify composables respond correctly to state changes and emit events
Do: Test that UI updates on state change and callbacks are invoked
Don't: Only test static rendering
Code Good: var clicked = false; composeTestRule.setContent { Button(onClick = { clicked = true }) { Text('Click') } }; composeTestRule.onNodeWithText('Click').performClick(); assertTrue(clicked)
Code Bad: // Only testing initial render

Description: Preview different states and configurations during development
Do: Create previews for loading error success and different screen sizes
Don't: Only preview happy path
Code Good: @Preview @Composable fun LoadingPreview() { TraderCard(UiState.Loading) } @Preview @Composable fun SuccessPreview() { TraderCard(UiState.Success(mockTrader)) } @Preview @Composable fun ErrorPreview() { TraderCard(UiState.Error('Failed')) }
Code Bad: @Preview @Composable fun Preview() { TraderCard(mockData) }

Description: PreviewParameterProvider generates multiple previews from data variations
Do: Use PreviewParameter to preview with different data sets
Don't: Duplicate preview functions for each variation
Code Good: class TraderPreviewProvider : PreviewParameterProvider<Trader> { override val values = sequenceOf(topTrader, newTrader, inactiveTrader) } @Preview @Composable fun Preview(@PreviewParameter(TraderPreviewProvider::class) trader: Trader) { TraderCard(trader) }
Code Bad: @Preview @Composable fun Preview1() { TraderCard(trader1) } @Preview @Composable fun Preview2() { TraderCard(trader2) }

Description: Test appearance on different devices and in dark mode
Do: Add device and uiMode parameters to previews
Don't: @Preview(device = Devices.PIXEL_4) @Preview(device = Devices.TABLET) @Preview(uiMode = Configuration.UI_MODE_NIGHT_YES) @Composable fun Preview() { ... }
Code Good: @Preview @Composable fun Preview() { ... } // Single configuration
Code Bad: info

Description: Hilt provides compile-time DI with good Compose integration
Do: Use hiltViewModel() to inject ViewModels in composables
Don't: Manually create ViewModels or use service locators
Code Good: @Composable fun TraderScreen(viewModel: TraderViewModel = hiltViewModel()) { val traders by viewModel.traders.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() }
Code Bad: @Composable fun TraderScreen() { val viewModel = remember { TraderViewModel(Repository()) } }

Description: Use appropriate scopes for dependencies based on their lifetime
Do: Scope repositories as singletons and ViewModels to their navigation scope
Don't: Create new instances everywhere or scope everything as singleton
Code Good: @Singleton class TraderRepository @Inject constructor(); @HiltViewModel class TraderViewModel @Inject constructor(repo: TraderRepository)
Code Bad: // Creating new repository instance in every ViewModel

Description: Provide meaningful descriptions for screen readers
Do: Add contentDescription to images and icons
Don't: Ship UI without accessibility descriptions
Code Good: Icon(Icons.Default.Star, contentDescription = 'Favorite')
Code Bad: Icon(Icons.Default.Star, contentDescription = null)

Description: Configure semantics for better accessibility support
Do: Set appropriate semantic roles and actions
Don't: Ignore semantic configuration
Code Good: Button(onClick = action, modifier = Modifier.semantics { role = Role.Button }) { Text('Submit') }
Code Bad: // Custom clickable without proper semantics Modifier.clickable { action() }

Description: Ensure UI adapts to increased font sizes
Do: Test with largest accessibility font sizes and handle text overflow
Don't: Use fixed text sizes or clip text at large sizes
Code Good: Text(text, maxLines = 2, overflow = TextOverflow.Ellipsis, style = MaterialTheme.typography.bodyLarge)
Code Bad: Text(text, fontSize = 14.sp, maxLines = 1) // Fixed size clips with large fonts

Description: animate*AsState provides easy animated value changes
Do: Use animateDpAsState animateColorAsState for simple property animations
Don't: Manually manage animation state for simple value changes
Code Good: val size by animateDpAsState(targetValue = if (expanded) 200.dp else 100.dp, animationSpec = spring())
Code Bad: var size by remember { mutableStateOf(100.dp) }; LaunchedEffect(expanded) { /* Manually animate */ }

Description: AnimatedVisibility provides easy show/hide animations
Do: Use AnimatedVisibility for conditional content with animations
Don't: Manually animate alpha and size for show/hide
Code Good: AnimatedVisibility(visible = showDetails, enter = fadeIn() + expandVertically(), exit = fadeOut() + shrinkVertically()) { DetailsContent() }
Code Bad: if (showDetails) { DetailsContent() } // No animation

Description: Organize related screens and components into feature modules
Do: Create separate modules for distinct feature areas
Don't: Put all composables in a single module
Code Good: // :feature:trader module contains TraderListScreen TraderDetailScreen TraderCard etc
Code Bad: // All screens in :app module

Description: Use AndroidView for legacy Views in Compose and ComposeView for Compose in Views
Do: Use AndroidView with proper update callback for View interop
Don't: Ignore View lifecycle when using AndroidView
Code Good: AndroidView(factory = { context -> MapView(context) }, update = { mapView -> mapView.setLocation(location) })
Code Bad: AndroidView(factory = { MapView(it).apply { setLocation(location) } }) // No updates
